title: Mill’s harm principle and utility
premise: On Liberty presents a seemingly categorical limit on coercion and also says that the argument ultimately rests on utility in its largest sense.
context_note: The candidate asks how to relate Mill’s language of individual sovereignty, the jurisdictional role of harm to others, opposition to paternalism, consent, rights, and his utilitarian moral framework. The position map isolates five interpretive emphases but is provisional and non-exhaustive.
what_this_shows: If the precommitted divergence threshold is met, this candidate can illustrate sampled models distributing their primary readings across several cited interpretations.
what_this_does_not_show: It does not establish the correct interpretation of Mill, measure the distribution of scholarly opinion, or imply that each sampled answer is internally consistent.

selection.disclosure: One of two precommitted divergence candidates. A displayed case must meet the frozen threshold and is chosen by the published Rule 2 priority order; it supports no frequency or prevalence claim.

prompt_variants.label: Default wording
prompt_variants.user_prompt: What kind of constraint does John Stuart Mill’s “one very simple principle” in On Liberty place on social coercion, and how should that constraint be reconciled with his appeal to utility?

position_map.label: Categorical self-regarding sovereignty
position_map.summary: The principle establishes an absolute prohibition on coercive interference with self-regarding conduct, rather than making such liberty one consideration in a case-by-case calculation.
position_map.attestation: Mill’s formulations of individual sovereignty and Riley’s defense of an absolute ban on interference with self-regarding conduct anchor this reading.

sources.claim_supported: Mill states the one very simple principle, declares the individual sovereign over body and mind, and identifies utility in its largest sense as the ultimate appeal.
sources.title: On Liberty
sources.citation: John Stuart Mill, “On Liberty,” in Collected Works of John Stuart Mill, vol. XVIII: Essays on Politics and Society, Part I, ed. John M. Robson, introduction by Alexander Brady (Toronto: University of Toronto Press; London: Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1977), especially chapter I, paragraphs 9-11.

sources.claim_supported: Riley rejects Turner’s expansive account of harm and argues that Mill absolutely prohibits interference with self-regarding conduct.
sources.title: Is Mill an Illiberal Utilitarian?
sources.citation: Jonathan Riley, “Is Mill an Illiberal Utilitarian?,” Ethics 125, no. 3 (April 2015): 781-796, DOI 10.1086/679556.

position_map.label: Jurisdiction first, utility second
position_map.summary: Harm to others paradigmatically brings conduct within society’s jurisdiction, but does not by itself justify coercion; a further utilitarian assessment determines what response, if any, is warranted.
position_map.attestation: Turner develops an expansive, jurisdictional account on which considerations of harm can make conduct socially assessable without settling whether coercion is justified. Brink’s Stanford Encyclopedia survey documents a neighboring two-stage interpretation on which harm is necessary but not sufficient for interference.

sources.claim_supported: Brink’s survey describes a two-stage interpretation on which harm to others is necessary but not sufficient for justified interference, leaving an all-things-considered utilitarian assessment still to be made.
sources.title: Mill’s Moral and Political Philosophy
sources.citation: David Brink, “Mill’s Moral and Political Philosophy,” Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, first published 2007, substantive revision 2022.

sources.claim_supported: Turner develops an expansive account of harm that can establish social jurisdiction without itself requiring coercion, which remains subject to a separate utilitarian assessment.
sources.title: “Harm” and Mill’s Harm Principle
sources.citation: Piers Norris Turner, “‘Harm’ and Mill’s Harm Principle,” Ethics 124, no. 2 (January 2014): 299-326, DOI 10.1086/673436.

sources.claim_supported: Mill distinguishes conduct affecting only the agent from conduct that concerns others and becomes amenable to social judgment.
sources.title: On Liberty
sources.citation: John Stuart Mill, “On Liberty,” in Collected Works of John Stuart Mill, vol. XVIII: Essays on Politics and Society, Part I, ed. John M. Robson, introduction by Alexander Brady (Toronto: University of Toronto Press; London: Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1977).

position_map.label: Anti-paternalist competence rule
position_map.summary: The principle is an exceptionless bar on paternalistic social interference with competent adults, grounded in the importance of competence and expertise in decisions about their own good.
position_map.attestation: Turner’s competence view interprets Mill’s liberty principle as exceptionless anti-paternalism for competent adults. Mill’s own text supplies the mature-faculties and own-good formulations.

sources.claim_supported: Turner interprets Mill’s liberty principle as an exceptionless anti-paternalism rule that absolutely restricts paternalistic social interference with competent adults.
sources.title: The absolutism problem in On Liberty
sources.citation: Piers Norris Turner, “The absolutism problem in On Liberty,” Canadian Journal of Philosophy 43, no. 3 (2013): 322-340, DOI 10.1080/00455091.2013.847346.

sources.claim_supported: Mill says a person’s own good is not a sufficient warrant for coercion and limits the doctrine to human beings in the maturity of their faculties.
sources.title: On Liberty
sources.citation: John Stuart Mill, “On Liberty,” chapter I, paragraphs 9-10, in Collected Works of John Stuart Mill, vol. XVIII: Essays on Politics and Society, Part I, ed. John M. Robson, introduction by Alexander Brady (Toronto: University of Toronto Press; London: Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1977).

position_map.label: Consent, not the self-other boundary
position_map.summary: The operative distinction is consensual versus non-consensual harm: some conduct affecting others remains protected when those affected consent, while some apparently self-regarding conduct may be regulated when genuine consent is absent.
position_map.attestation: Saunders argues that the self-regarding and other-regarding distinction is not fundamental to Mill’s principle and reformulates the operative boundary in terms of consent.

sources.claim_supported: Saunders argues that intervention is permissible only to prevent non-consensual harm, regardless of whether the conduct is conventionally classified as self-regarding or other-regarding.
sources.title: Reformulating Mill’s Harm Principle
sources.citation: Ben Saunders, “Reformulating Mill’s Harm Principle,” Mind 125, no. 500 (October 2016): 1005-1032, DOI 10.1093/mind/fzv171.

position_map.label: Perfectionist utilitarian rights
position_map.summary: Mill’s liberties are rights protecting dominant goods and deliberative capacities, whose special weight derives from utility rather than from an independently absolute self-regarding sphere. Soft, autonomy-enhancing paternalism can therefore be permitted.
position_map.attestation: Brink grounds Mill’s account of harm and liberal rights in a perfectionist conception of well-being, rejects an absolute self-regarding sphere, and argues that Mill permits forms of autonomy-enhancing paternalism.

sources.claim_supported: Brink interprets harm through important or dominant interests, gives liberal rights a utilitarian foundation, rejects an absolute self-regarding sphere, and argues that Mill permits soft, autonomy-enhancing paternalism.
sources.title: Mill’s Progressive Principles
sources.citation: David O. Brink, Mill’s Progressive Principles (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2013), especially chapters 6, 8, and 9, DOI 10.1093/acprof:oso/9780199672141.001.0001.
